coredata.dc:title: Li-O2 flow batteries with high mass loading and energy density
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Author. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Xianglin
coredata.dc:description: LiO2 and Li-air batteries are promising energy storage technologies due to their high theoretical energy density. However, their achievable energy densities are much lower due to the low mass loading of electrodes and low electrode utilization rates. This study designed LiO2 flow batteries that actively circulate liquid electrolytes through the porous positive electrode, enabling high utilization rates for the electrodes with high mass loading. The experimental tests and model simulations in this study demonstrated the advantages of the flow battery design. Using commercially available carbon electrodes and 1 M LiTFSI in tetraethylene glycol dimethyl ether (TEGDME), this scalable battery concept achieved an energy density of 773 Wh/kg at 0.25 mA/cm2 and 436.6 Wh/kg at 0.5 mA/cm2 at room temperature. In comparison, using the same electrodes and electrolytes, traditional LiO2 batteries without electrolyte flow only obtained 294 Wh/kg at 0.25 mA/cm2 and 239.7 Wh/kg at 0.5 mA/cm2. In addition, the flow battery demonstrated better cyclability compared to the traditional battery. The flow battery completed 23 cycles at a current density of 0.25 mA/cm2 and a cut-off capacity of 1 mAh/cm2, whereas the traditional passive battery completed only 8 cycles under the same conditions. The model simulation quantitatively showed that convection of the electrolyte significantly improved mass transfer and material utilization of the positive electrode. The energy consumption to circulate the electrolyte was measured to be only 1.3 % of the discharged energy. Comparable improvements in energy density were observed when 1 M LiTFSI in dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) was utilized as the electrolyte. This design opens up opportunities for new research to develop scalable batteries with exceptionally high energy density, far surpassing current state-of-the-art Li-ion batteries, while maintaining relatively high power density.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Rechargeable lithium battery
Flow battery
High energy density
High power density
High mass loading
Electrochemical modeling

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The author greatly appreciates the funding support by the Advanced Research Projects Agency-Energy (ARPA-E) PROPEL-1K program (Award DE-AR0001884) and the National Science Foundation (Award 1941083 and 2329821). The author also wants to acknowledge Helen Ma for conducting XPS and the Raman spectrum experiments.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of a typical LiO2 battery.
Top views of current collectors with (a) interdigitated and (b) serpentine channels.
Computational domain and boundary conditions of a lithium-O2 battery.

Comparisons of deep discharge-charge cycles of active (interdigitated) and passive (serpentine) cells using O2 at room temperature with 1 M LiTFSI in TEGDME. The active and passive cells are charged at 0.25 mA/cm2 and discharged at (a) 0.25 mA/cm2 and (b) 0.5 mA/cm2.
(a) Time variations of pressure at the inlet of the active battery with a flow rate of 1 M LiTFSI in TEGDME at 0.5 mL/min, and (b) pressure drops at different flow rates.

Cyclability tests of (a) active battery and (b) passive battery using 1 M LiTFSI in TEGDME under the current density of 0.25 mA/cm2. The flow rate of the active cell is 0.5 mL/min. (c) The comparison of capacities with the number of cycles.
Comparisons of experimentally measured and numerically simulated passive and active batteries at the discharge current density of 0.25 mA/cm2 using 1 M LiTFSI in TEGDME and O2.

(a) Pressure distribution and velocity vector plots of active cells with 5 kPa pressure difference between adjacent channels and (b) volume fraction of Li2O2, ε Li2O2, at the end of discharge of the active cell (14.2 mAh/cm2). The volume fraction of Li2O2, ε Li2O2, (c) of the passive cell at the end of discharge (7.4 mAh/cm2) and (d) the active cell at the same discharge capacity of 7.4 mAh/cm2.
LSV of active cells at 0.1 mV/s between 2.0 and 4.5 V at 25 °C using 1 M LiTFSI in TEGDME or 1 M LiTFSI in DMSO.

Comparisons of deep discharge-charge cycles of active (interdigitated) and passive (serpentine) cells using O2 at room temperature using 1 M LiTFSI in DMSO: (a) discharged at 0.25 mA/cm2 and (b) discharged at 0.5 mA/cm2. All cells are charged at 0.25 mA/cm2.

row.entry: Active area of electrode per volume

head.ce:title: Li-O2 flow batteries with high mass loading and energy density

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mechanical Engineering and Materials Science, Washington University in St Louis, St Louis, MO 63130, USA

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Mechanical Engineering and Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Mechanical Engineering and Materials Science, Washington University in St Louis, St Louis, MO, USA 63130

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mechanical Engineering, University of Kansas, Lawrence, KS 66045, USA
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Mechanical Engineering, University of Kansas, Lawrence, KS, USA 66045

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Mechanical Engineering and Materials Science, Washington University in St Louis, St Louis, MO 63130, USA.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Mechanical Engineering and Materials Science

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: LiO2 and Li-air batteries are promising energy storage technologies due to their high theoretical energy density. However, their achievable energy densities are much lower due to the low mass loading of electrodes and low electrode utilization rates. This study designed LiO2 flow batteries that actively circulate liquid electrolytes through the porous positive electrode, enabling high utilization rates for the electrodes with high mass loading. The experimental tests and model simulations in this study demonstrated the advantages of the flow battery design. Using commercially available carbon electrodes and 1 M LiTFSI in tetraethylene glycol dimethyl ether (TEGDME), this scalable battery concept achieved an energy density of 773 Wh/kg at 0.25 mA/cm2 and 436.6 Wh/kg at 0.5 mA/cm2 at room temperature. In comparison, using the same electrodes and electrolytes, traditional LiO2 batteries without electrolyte flow only obtained 294 Wh/kg at 0.25 mA/cm2 and 239.7 Wh/kg at 0.5 mA/cm2. In addition, the flow battery demonstrated better cyclability compared to the traditional battery. The flow battery completed 23 cycles at a current density of 0.25 mA/cm2 and a cut-off capacity of 1 mAh/cm2, whereas the traditional passive battery completed only 8 cycles under the same conditions.

The model simulation quantitatively showed that convection of the electrolyte significantly improved mass transfer and material utilization of the positive electrode. The energy consumption to circulate the electrolyte was measured to be only 1.3 % of the discharged energy. Comparable improvements in energy density were observed when 1 M LiTFSI in dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) was utilized as the electrolyte. This design opens up opportunities for new research to develop scalable batteries with exceptionally high energy density, far surpassing current state-of-the-art Li-ion batteries, while maintaining relatively high power density.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para:
LiO2 and Li-air batteries share very similar challenges. Therefore, this manuscript will consistently use the term of “Li-O2 battery” to represent both LiO2 and Li-air batteries. Fig. 1 shows a sketch of a LiO2. The electrochemical reactions in the negative (Li) electrode, positive (O2) electrode, and overall reaction are:
(1)
Negative : Li ↔
Li +
+
e −
,
(2)
Positive :
2e −
+
2Li +
+
O 2
↔
Li 2
O 2
solid
,
(3)
Overall : 2Li +
O 2
↔
Li 2
O 2
2.96
V
.

The active materials only include Li and O2. As a result, the theoretical specific energy calculated by the weight of these two active materials is extremely high (3.46 kWh/kg) [1,2]. However, many other necessary components are required to operate LiO2 batteries. These include separator, electrolyte, positive electrode, current collector, gaskets, flow channels, and balance of plant (BOP). Even when we only consider the minimum weight required at the cell level, which excludes BOP, the achievable specific energy is significantly lower than 3.46 kWh/kg [3].

LiO2 batteries require flow channels to distribute O2 to the electrode. Flow channels add significant volume and weight to each unit cell. There are very limited studies of flow channel designs applied in metal-air batteries. However, we can anticipate that channels for metal-air batteries will resemble those used in fuel cells [4], redox flow batteries [5], and electrolyzers [6]. As a rough estimate, we assume flow channels could have a thickness as slim as 0.5 mm and a weight as light as 100 mg/cm2. Once the weight of the flow channel is considered, the theoretical specific energy is significantly lower than 3.46 kWh/kg, especially when a thin Li chip is used. To achieve a theoretical specific energy of 1 kWh/kg, the Li chip must be at least 0.25 mm thick. Other rechargeable batteries, including Li-ion batteries, also need to balance the thickness of the negative and positive electrodes to increase the achievable specific energy [7,8]. Since Li-ion batteries have low areal capacity and do not require flow channels, using thin lithium metal (≤30 μm) is crucial for attaining high specific energy [9]. However, this differs in LiO2 batteries due to their distinct cell structures and design priorities.

The Supporting Information (SI) includes the analysis of mass balance and thickness (or volume) balance of cell components when a 0.25-mm thick Li chip is used as the anode of a LiO2 battery. The weight breakdown of each cell component shows that the weight of the flow channel (100 mg/cm2) is comparable to the combined weight of all other cell components (102.6 mg/cm2), including the lithium chip, separator, electrolyte, oxygen, and positive electrode. Therefore, the weight of the flow channel significantly impacts the specific energy of LiO2 batteries. As also shown in the SI, the thickness and weight of the positive electrode and the specific energy of the cell also highly depend on the porosity of the positive electrode. Increasing the porosity decreases the weight of the positive electrode, thereby increasing the specific energy of the cell.

Similarly, the thickness of the flow channel (0.5 mm) is comparable to the combined weight of all other cell components (0.6 mm). As could also be expected, the thickness of the positive electrode and the energy density of the cell highly depend on the porosity of the positive electrode. Increasing the porosity decreases the thickness of the positive electrode since the pore volume should be sufficient to host all generated Li2O2 solids during discharge. The decrease of the positive electrode thickness increases the energy density of the cell.

As demonstrated in the analysis, the positive electrode is likely to be 0.3 mm or thicker in order to achieve high specific energy and energy density. However, oxygen transfer within thick positive electrodes, which are typically saturated with liquid electrolytes, will be extremely challenging. The diffusivity of O2 in TEGDME and DMSO are compared in Table S1 [10]. This study will utilize TEGDME and DMSO as the battery electrolytes, since they are among the most commonly used organic electrolytes in LiO2 battery research due to their relatively high electrochemical stability [11,12]. Additionally, these electrolytes have markedly different physical and chemical properties, such as viscosity, O2 diffusivity, solubility, and surface tension. Therefore, comparing these electrolytes will offer valuable insights into the significant impact of mass transfer in LiO2 batteries. The low diffusivity of O2 in liquid electrolytes limits the operating current density of LiO2 batteries to <1 mA/cm2. A 0.25-mm thick Li chip has an areal capacity of 51.59 mAh/cm2 in theory. The current density to complete 1C discharge and charge is 51.59 mA/cm2, which is more than two orders of magnitude higher than typical current densities applied in LiO2 research [13,14].

The SI computes the local concentration and mass flux of O2 within the porous positive electrode using a 1D non-reactive mass diffusion model. Understanding the local O2 concentration and flux helps to determine the theoretically maximum operating current density. These analytical solutions help to quantify the performance limitation caused by the variation of electrolyte properties. The analysis separately focuses on the impact of initial O2 concentration and the “steady state” mass flux.

As demonstrated in Figs. S5 and S6, the transient current density and limiting current density are limited by the mass transfer of O2. Batteries using electrolytes with higher oxygen diffusivity can achieve higher operating current density. Additionally, the current density decreases as the electrode thickness increases. The analyses in the SI use an electrode thickness of 0.32 mm. According to the non-dimensionalized equations in the SI, the current density is inversely proportional to the electrode thickness.

When thick positive electrodes or electrodes with high mass loadings are used, the positive electrode's limited O2 transfer and ionic conductivity restrict the battery's capacity and current rate. Beyond a certain mass loading or electrode thickness, further increases in mass loading only marginally enhance the areal capacity [15]. Additionally, batteries designed for high current and power rates necessitate positive electrodes with improved O2 transport [16]. Considerable efforts have been dedicated to developing positive electrodes with high mass loading for LiO2 batteries to achieve high areal capacity.

Y. Lin et al. [17] developed graphene-based O2 electrodes with high mass loadings, up to 10 mg/cm2. The binder-free O2 electrodes were prepared using compression-molding holey graphene materials. The battery achieved an areal capacity of 35.3 mAh/cm2 at 0.2 mA/cm2. W. Yu et al. [18] used porous graphene to develop binder-free graphene mesosponge sheets as the positive electrode of LiO2 batteries. The electrode has a hierarchical porous graphene-wall structure with controlled pore sizes between angstrom and submillimeter. Together with the hierarchically porous electrode, this study used electrolyte mixed by 0.5 mol/L LiTFSI, 0.5 mol/L lithium nitrate (LiNO3), and 0.1 mol/L 2,2,6,6tetramethylpiperidinyloxyl (TEMPO) dissolved in TEGDME. LiNO3 effectively protects the Li metal anode, and TEMPO acts as a redox mediator to reduce the charge potential. The battery obtained an areal capacity of >30 mAh/cm2.

H. Song et al. [19] developed ultrathick (0.7–3.4 mm) battery cathodes from carbonized and activated natural basswood. Ru nanoparticles with size <5 nm are deposited on the porous surface of microchannels of the wood-derived carbon host. The microchannels have low tortuosity and high open ratio, which facilitate expedited oxygen gas diffusion. The 3.4-mm thick electrode obtained an impressive areal capacity of 56.0 mAh cm−2 at 0.1 mA cm−2. The calculated specific capacities are 5591 mAh gRu −1 and 520 mAh gcathode −1.

S.H. Park et al. [20] developed a double oxygen supply system to deliver oxygen from both the cathode opening and the separator to significantly improve the utilization of the cathode electrode, which increases the capacity and reduces the overpotential. The cathode electrode made from carbon nanotube (CNT) film with a loading of 6 mg cm−2 and a thickness of 153 μm obtained the areal capacity of 46.93 mAh cm−2 (4757 mAh g−1) at 0.6 mA cm−2 (100 A g−1). The Li2O2 deposition rates at different vertical positions were quantified using TiOSO4-based titration analyses. Results confirmed that improved oxygen transfer maximized the utilization of cathode electrodes with high mass loading.

M. Jenkins et al. [21] replaced the PTFE binder with a copolymer of 4,5-difluoro-2,2-bis(trifluoromethyl) − 1,3-dioxole and tetrafluoroethylene. The copolymer has a high O2 diffusivity to improve O2 transfer in a 300-μm thick electrode. The battery delivered a discharge capacity of 31 mAh cm−2 at the current density of 1 mA/cm2 using O2. No charge performance was demonstrated in the study. This study also emphasized that cells with thick electrodes require improved O2 transport.

Meanwhile, numerous studies have reviewed research efforts on developing high-loading cathodes for Li-ion batteries [22], metal-air, and metal‑sulfur batteries [23–25]. Optimizing electrode design to enhance mass transfer and electrode utilization is crucial. Many of these studies emphasize improving the utilization of the electrode by increasing the mass and ion transfer properties [26]. Creating a continuous conductive network of active material (for electrical transfer), binder, and electrolyte (for ion transfer) is essential for achieving high-performance electrodes. Similar structural designs featuring low tortuosity and high permeability are also preferred in LiO2 batteries.

This study experimentally tested and numerically simulated a flow battery design that incorporated convection within the positive electrode to enhance O2 mass transfer. Instead of focusing on developing positive electrodes using customized materials, our approach offers a straightforward solution to significantly improve the material utilization rates of positive electrodes with high mass loadings. This LiO2 flow cell design leverages commercially available electrodes and materials, enabling the fabrication of low-cost, scalable battery packs with high operating current density and capacity. Additionally, model simulations are included to corroborate the experimental results.

Convection of electrolytes saturated with O2 could significantly enhance the mass transfer of both O2 and lithium ions, thereby increasing the operating current density and capacity of the battery. We have designed specialized cells and channels to enable precise control of electrolyte convection, demonstrating performance improvements compared to traditional LiO2 cells that rely on the slow diffusion of O2 in liquid electrolytes.

To introduce the convection of electrolytes within the positive electrode, we designed and fabricated current collectors with an interdigitated flow channel (Fig. 2 (a)). The interdigitated channel has an overall size of 1.3 × 1.3 cm2, with a width and depth of 1 mm. During operation, the pressure difference between the inlet and outlet will push the liquid electrolyte through the positive electrolyte. The electrolyte flow rate can be precisely controlled by adjusting the pressure difference between the inlet and outlet. For comparison, we also designed and fabricated a serpentine channel (Fig. 2 (b)) of the same size as the interdigitated channel. The serpentine channel is supplied with O2 gas, which diffuses into the porous positive electrode. In passive cells using a serpentine channel, the liquid electrolyte remains stationary. All current collectors are made of highly corrosion-resistant 316 stainless steel [27].

Tetra ethylene glycol dimethyl ether (TEGDME, 99 %), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), and Bis(trifluoromethane)sulfonimide lithium salt (LiTFSI, 99.95 %) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Both solvents and salt were dried in a vacuum oven (Across International AT19-500C.220) at 40 °C for 12 h before use. Battery electrolytes were prepared in an argon-filled glovebox (Vacuum Technology Corp.), where H2O and O2 levels were maintained below 0.01 ppm. The appropriate amount of LiTFSI was dissolved in TEGDME or DMSO using a magnetic stirrer for at least 2 h to obtain a homogeneous solution with a concentration of 1 mol/L (1 M).

The battery comprises a negative current collector and a positive flow channel, which also serves as the current collector, a positive electrode with a diameter of 1.90 cm (3/4”), a Celgard 2500 separator from Fisher Scientific with a diameter of 2.54 cm (1”), and a lithium chip with a thickness of 0.25 mm and diameter of 1.6 cm as a negative electrode. As the positive electrodes, we utilized commercially available electrodes (ELAT LT2400W) purchased from the Fuel Cell Store. These electrodes are fabricated from carbon cloth coated with a mixture of PTFE binder and Vulcan XC-72R as the microporous layer (MPL). This commercial electrode has a thickness of 490 μm and a mass loading of 25 mg/cm2.

The separator was soaked in the electrolyte to ensure it was fully wetted before assembly. About 45 μL of electrolyte was injected into the carbon electrode and spread evenly across its surface until no excess liquid remained. PTFE gaskets were used to separate the negative and positive current collectors. The thickness of the gaskets was selected to achieve a compression ratio of approximately 80 % for the internal components. The cell was assembled and properly tightened in a Vacuum Technology Inc. glovebox filled with argon and with H2O and O2 of <0.01 ppm to prevent any leakage before being taken out for electrochemical testing.

The assembled cells were connected to a four-channel battery tester, Squidstat Prime from Admiral Instruments. For passive cells, pure oxygen was supplied at a flow rate of 3 standard cubic centimeters per minute (sccm). For active cells, liquid electrolyte saturated with O2 was supplied at a flow rate of 0.1 mL/min. All passive and active cells were assembled in the glovebox. Passive cells were purged with pure oxygen for three hours under open-circuit voltage (OCV). Active cells were purged with electrolytes saturated with O2 for one hour under OCV. All tests were conducted at room temperature, and a constant current density (0.25 or 0.5 mA/cm2) was applied during discharge, and a constant current density of 0.25 mA/cm2 was applied during charge. The discharge and charge cut-off voltages were set to 2 V and 4.5 V, respectively.

Batteries were tested with the curtailed capacity of 1.0 mAh cm−2. All specific capacity tests were carried out at 0.25 mA/cm2 current density. The discharge and charge cutoff voltages used were 2.0 V and 4.5 V, respectively.
The summary of experiments carried out in this study is shown in Fig. S7. All batteries use identical negative electrodes, positive electrodes, and separators.

We have developed CFD models using the properties of the electrolyte, electrode, and operating current densities to obtain fluid and mass transfer distributions. The results from these models could provide additional insights into the improvements in electrochemical performance. Details of the models are described below, and more information can be found in previous publications [10,28,29].

This model study considered 2D mass transfer and electrochemical reaction within the positive electrode of a Li-O2 battery. The model solved distributions of oxygen concentration, Li+ concentration, reaction rate, and volume fraction of solid product. The model assumes the electrolyte fully fills pores in the positive electrode. In both active and passive batteries, oxygen and lithium-ion transfer is only in the electrolyte. In addition, Li2O2 is assumed to deposit as a smooth film in the electrode [28]. The computational domain of the model includes the electrolyte and the positive O2 electrode, as shown within the dashed line in Fig. 3 .
The model solves the following governing equations interatively since parameters and coefficients change during each time step.

Momentum Equation
(4)

(8)

(9)

Boundaries for the computational domain are numbered in Fig. 3. The upper (I) and lower (II) boundaries are considered to be symmetric boundaries, and the corresponding boundary conditions are:

The flux of oxygen at boundary III is 0. At the electrode/O2 interface (boundary IV), the flux of lithium ion is 0, and the oxygen concentration is set as the saturation value:

The room temperature is applied throughout the computational domain and all boundaries.

The computational grids were generated based on the finite volume method (FVM) [30], and the governing equations were discretized and solved by a code developed at home using Fortran. The grid number is 125 × 20, and the time step is 1 s. The code has undergone mesh and time step independence studies using meshes ranging from (25 × 4) to (250 × 40) and time steps ranging from 0.2 to 5 s. All the model results are both grid-independent and the time step independent. After the results are converged in each time step, the cell voltage is determined by the following equation:
(16)
V =
E 0
– η – I ×
δ EL
/
σ EL
,
where E 0 is the thermodynamic equilibrium voltage, δ EL is the thickness of the separator, and σ EL is the conductivity of the electrolyte. Values of parameters used in the model are all presented in Table 1 .

We started by experimentally measuring and comparing batteries using a commercial positive electrode (ELAT LT2400W) and TEGDME as the electrolytes. The performance difference between passive and active batteries demonstrated the importance of mass transfer and the significance of convection in improving the discharge capacity and voltage of the batteries. We then applied the models to quantitatively simulate the distributions of O2 concentration, velocities, and volume fraction of Li2O2 to clearly explain the critical roles of mass transfer in battery discharge. This is followed by similar tests using a commercial ELAT LT2400W electrode and DMSO as the electrolyte. Since DMSO has much lower oxygen solubility [31] (3.3 × 10−4 vs. 8.57 × 10−4 mol/L) and diffusivity [31,32] (1.33 × 10−5 vs. 2.60 × 10−5 cm2/s) than TEGDME, the contributions of diffusion and convection to the discharge performances could be very different in these two electrolytes.

Fig. 4 (a) compares the performance of batteries when they are both discharged at 0.25 mA/cm2 and charged at 0.25 mA/cm2 using 1 M LiTFSI in TEGDME as the electrolyte. The active battery obtains a specific capacity of 19.42 mAh/cm2, while the passive battery obtains an area capacity of only 7.17 mAh/cm2 at the current density of 0.25 mA/cm2. Results in Fig. 4 show that the convection of electrolytes saturated with O2 has more than doubled (2.7 times) the discharge capacity. The improvement of mass transfer increases the utilization of the positive electrode and the discharge capacity. This experimental result will be confirmed by the model simulations to be discussed later.

Both batteries used a 250-um-thick Li chip as the negative electrode, which has a theoretical capacity of 51.59 mAh/cm2. Therefore, 23.5 % of the Li chip in the anode has been utilized during the discharge of the active cell. In comparison, only 13.9 % of the Li chip has been used during the discharge of the passive cell. The calculated discharge energy densities are 46.90 mWh/cm2 and 18.0 mWh/cm2, respectively. The weights of the main components in both batteries include 13.35 mg/cm2 of Li chip, 1.35 mg/cm2 of separator, 25 mg/cm2 of positive electrode, and 15.19 mg/cm2 of electrolyte. The weights of consumed O2 during discharge are 11.60 and 4.28 mg/cm2 for active and passive batteries, respectively. The total weight of the active battery (Li, separator, positive electrode, electrolyte, and O2) is 66.49 mg/cm2, and the total weight of the passive cell is 61.17 mg/cm2. The calculated specific energies of the active and passive batteries, without considering the weight of the current collector and the balance of the plant, are 705 and 294 Wh/kg, respectively. If the current collector can be optimized with minimum thickness (0.5 mm) and weight (100 mg/cm2), the specific energies of the active and passive cells are 282 and 112 Wh/kg, respectively.

When the discharge current density was increased to 0.5 mA/cm2 (Fig. 4 (b)), the higher current density led to a faster O2 consumption and more challenging mass transfer. Consequently, the discharge capacities of both active and passive cells decreased. However, the active cell still achieved twice the discharge capacity of the passive cell (11.44 vs. 5.80 mAh/cm2). Considering the weight of each component, the corresponding specific energies are 437 and 240 Wh/kg. After adding the weight of the flow channel, which is estimated to be 100 mg/cm2, the specific energies are 167 and 88 Wh/kg for the active and passive batteries.

After disassembling the active cell after the deep discharge-charge tests, we found that no lithium metal remained, even though our calculations indicated that less than a quarter of the 250 μm thick lithium chip had been consumed. Instead, a thin layer of semitransparent gel-like substance is observed on the negative electrode, likely resulting from the reaction of lithium metal with water and CO2 from the ambient air. The remaining material was dried and analyzed using Raman and XPS techniques, revealing that it primarily consists of Li2CO3 and LiOH (as detailed in the Supplementary Information). This substrate cannot strip or deposit lithium metal, which terminates the battery's life. The results demonstrated the importance of lithium protection. Future studies aiming to extend battery lifetime must focus on protecting the positive electrode and preventing contamination and mechanical breakdown due to significant volume changes during discharge-charge cycles.

To quantify the pressure drop and power consumption required to pump the electrolytes through the electrodes, we measured the pressure variations at the inlet of the battery using an Omega pressure sensor (Model No: PX409-050GUSBH, Accuracy: 0.08 % BSL). As the electrolyte is delivered through a syringe pump, the pump alternates between infusion and withdrawal modes with 10 mL of electrolyte to ensure continuous flow during the experiments. The inlet and outlet of the battery switched when the syringe pump changed the flow direction, as shown in Fig. 5 (a). Consequently, the pressure difference between the inlet and outlet of the battery is half the difference between the maximum and minimum pressure measured by the sensor. The estimated pressure drop at a flow rate of 0.5 mL/min was 1.95 kPa, resulting in a calculated pumping power of 1.625 × 10−5 W. The discharging power at a current density of 0.25 mA/cm2 for a 2-cm2 electrode was approximately 1.25 × 10−3 W (assuming an average voltage of 2.5 V). Therefore, the power consumption to deliver the electrolyte was only 1.3 % of the power generated by the battery. Additionally, by running the electrolyte at different flow rates between 0.05 and 0.9 mL/min, we determined the pressure drops at various flow rates, as shown in Fig. 5 (b).

The selection of flow rates needs to ensure an adequate O2 supply at the operating current. Table S1 indicates that the O2 solubility in the electrolyte (TEGDME) is 8.57 × 10−4 mol/L. At a flow rate of 0.5 mL/min, the calculated oxygen supply rate is 7.1 × 10−9 mol/s. For a 2-cm2 electrode operating at 0.5 mA/cm2, the oxygen consumption rate is 5.2 × 10−9 mol/s. This results in a stoichiometric ratio of 1.38. To support higher operating currents, the electrolyte flow rate would need to be increased proportionally. The electrolyte flow rate should also account for properties such as oxygen solubility. Utilizing electrolytes with higher oxygen solubility, such as ionic liquids [33,34], would allow for a smaller electrolyte flow rate to maintain the same stoichiometric ratio. Meanwhile, designing porous electrodes with higher permeability can further reduce the energy consumption to circulate the liquid electrolyte. The author's research lab has published multiple studies on simulating flow resistance (permeability) of porous media and designing porous structures with reduced flow resistance [35,36]. Future investigations employing customized electrodes will utilize substrates with high porosity and large pore sizes to markedly increase electrode permeability. Prospective materials under consideration include high-porosity carbon felt and porous films with well-aligned pore orientations and connectivity.

To confirm the rechargeability of the active batteries, we tested and compared the cyclability of active and passive batteries using 1 M LiTFSI in TEGDME as the electrolyte. Both cells were discharged and charged at a current density of 0.25 mA/cm2, using cutoff voltages of 2.0 V and 4.5 V. The results in Fig. 6 show that the active battery underwent significantly more cycles (23 cycles) compared to the passive battery (8 cycles) before reaching the cutoff voltages.

Active batteries have better cyclability than passive batteries due to their ability to effectively manage and utilize the electrode materials within the cell. The more uniform species distributions, which will be discussed in the following section, result in more consistent local current density and lower local overpotentials. The reduction of local overpotential alleviates the degradation of carbon and electrolyte at high voltages and minimizes the formation of detrimental byproducts during cycling. These improvements lead to better charge retention, reduced degradation over multiple cycles, and overall enhanced cyclability. Consequently, active batteries can sustain more charge-discharge cycles before reaching their cutoff voltages compared to passive batteries, which may experience quicker performance deterioration and less efficient electrochemical reactions.

It is important to note that the cyclability of LiO2 batteries is significantly lower than that of Li-ion batteries due to several factors:
•
The open system nature of LiO2 batteries makes them vulnerable to contamination from ambient substances such as H2O and CO2, which can negatively affect battery performance by reacting with the lithium anode and causing side reactions during ORR and OER processes. Protecting the lithium metal is critical for increasing the lifetime of LiO2 batteries.
•
This study utilized a high cut-off areal capacity and current density. The number of cycles would increase significantly with a decrease in current density and cut-off capacity.
•
This study employed a high cut-off voltage of 4.5 V during charging. Information in the supplementary information and Fig. S11 indicates that the battery life could be significantly improved by reducing the cut-off voltage to 4.25 V.

We compared the model simulations of the discharge of passive and active batteries at 0.25 mA/cm2 with O2, Fig. 7 , to validate the model. The pressure difference of two adjacent channels in an active cell is assumed to be 2.0 kPa. This pressure difference is consistent with the experimentally measured pressure drop observed during experiments using 1 M LiTFSI in TEGDME, as shown in Fig. 5 (a). The model simulations generally match the experimental data very well, as shown in Fig. 7. The average error between simulated and experimental voltages under the same capacity is calculated to be 6.1 %. This confirms that the model can capture the main factors determining the performance of both active and passive batteries.

We then utilize this experimentally validated model to gain insights into mass transfer, local reaction rates, and electrode utilization within porous electrodes. Fig. 8 (a) illustrates the pressure distributions and velocity vectors of the liquid electrolyte at the end of the discharge of the active cell. The average velocity is calculated to be 1.12 × 10−5 m/s. The increased O2 supply driven by convection results in more uniform local reaction rates and better electrode utilization, particularly in areas distant from the O2 channel. Consequently, the average volume fraction of Li2O2 product, which is proportional to the cumulative capacity of the cell, is significantly higher in the active battery (0.104) compared to the passive battery (0.055) at the end of discharge. Given that the porosity of the positive electrode is 0.6, the utilization rate of the positive electrode has improved from 8.8 % to 17.3 % after introducing convection of the electrolyte. Even when both batteries are discharged to the same capacity, leading to the same average volume fraction of Li2O2 (0.055), the local distribution of Li2O2 in the active cell (Fig. 8 (d)) is more uniform than in the passive cell (Fig. 8 (c)).

Given the lower oxygen solubility [31] (3.3 × 10−4 vs. 8.57 × 10−4 mol/L) and diffusivity [31,32] (1.33 × 10−5 vs. 2.60 × 10−5 cm2/s) of DMSO than TEGDME. LiO2 batteries using two different electrolytes could have different limiting current densities. We conducted linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) tests of batteries using identical positive electrodes and separators but different electrolytes. The voltages were scanned between OCV and 2 V (ORR) and between OCV and 4.5 V (OER), with one hour of resting between the ORR and OER scans. The voltage scan rates were both 0.1 mV/s, and the results are shown in Fig. 9 .

The electrode achieved peak ORR current densities of −3.88 and − 0.98 mA/cm2 and peak OER current densities of 1.59 and 0.43 mA/cm2 using 1 M LiTFSI in TEGDME and 1 M LiTFSI in DMSO as the electrolyte, respectively. The ratios of the ORR and OER current densities are 4.0 and 3.7, respectively. The ratio of the O2 diffusivity multiplying the O2 solubility in TEGDME and DMSO is about 5.1, which is similar to the ratio of the peak ORR. This further confirms that the mass transfer rate of O2 limits the peak ORR and OER current densities.

Since batteries using DMSO face more challenges of O2 supply, the impact of mass transfer improvement is more important in batteries using electrolytes with low O2 diffusivity and concentration. The following experiment compares the performance of batteries when they are discharged and charged at 0.25 and 0.5 mA/cm2 using 1 M LiTFSI in DMSO as the electrolyte.

When the cells were discharged at 0.25 mA/cm2 using 1 M LiTFSI in DMSO as the electrolyte (Fig. 10 (a)), the active cell obtained a specific capacity of 19.24 mAh/cm2, while the passive cell obtained the area capacity of only 8.83 mAh/cm2. The convection of electrolytes saturated with O2 has more than doubled (2.2×) the discharge capacity, similar to batteries using TEGDME as the electrolyte at 0.25 mA/cm2.

Both cells used a 250-um-thick Li chip negative electrode, which has a capacity of 51.59 mAh/cm2. Therefore, 23.31 % of the Li chip has been utilized during the discharge of the active cell. Meanwhile, 10.7 % of the Li chip was used during the discharge of the passive cell. The calculated discharge energy densities are 51.33 mWh/cm2 and 22.99 mWh/cm2. The weights of the main components in both cells include 13.35 mg/cm2 of Li chip, 1.35 mg/cm2 of separator, 25 mg/cm2 of positive electrode, and 15.19 mg/cm2 electrolyte. The weights of consumed O2 during discharge are 11.49 and 5.27 mg/cm2 for active and passive cells, respectively. The total weight of the active cell (Li, separator, positive electrode, electrolyte, and O2) is 66.38 mg/cm2 , and the total weight of the passive cell is 60.16 mg/cm2. The calculated specific energies of the active and passive cells, without considering the weight of the current collector and the balance of the plant, are 773 and 382 Wh/kg, respectively. If the current collector can be optimized with minimum thickness (0.5 mm) and weight (100 mg/cm2), the specific energies of the active and passive cells are 309 and 144 Wh/kg, respectively.

When the discharge current density is increased to 0.5 mA/cm2 (Fig. 10 (b)), the active cell achieves a specific capacity of 9.62 mAh/cm2, while the passive cell achieves an area capacity of only 4.45 mAh/cm2. The use of 1 M LiTFSI in DMSO as the electrolyte has an even more significant impact on increasing the discharge capacity (2.2 times) compared to 1 M LiTFSI in TEGDME as the electrolyte (2.0 times) when the electrolyte is circulated at the current density of 0.5 mA/cm2. Considering the weight of each component, the corresponding specific energies are 396 and 201 Wh/kg. After adding the weight of the flow channel, which is estimated to be 100 mg/cm2, the specific energies are 150 and 73 Wh/kg for the active and passive batteries. One notable difference in the result is that the charging capacities using 1 M LiTFSI in DMSO have been significantly improved compared to using 1 M LiTFSI in TEGDME as the solvent. For example, the charging capacities have been increased from 1.35 (TEGDME) to 14.33 mAh/cm2 (DMSO) at the current density of 0.25 mA/cm2. The results are consistent with findings from the literature that DMSO is more stable during the initial cycling of the battery [37,38]. Please note that the coulombic efficiencies during deep discharge-charge cycles are usually very low in LiO2 batteries [39]. Under normal operation, these batteries are typically operated under given cut-off capacities, before the voltage reaches the 2.0 V and 4.5 V cut-offs.

This study has experimentally measured and numerically simulated the critical role of mass transfer in LiO2 batteries, significantly advancing our understanding and application of these systems. The novel battery design, with liquid electrolytes actively circulated through the porous positive electrode, significantly enhances total and areal discharge capacities while optimizing the utilization of porous positive electrodes. Utilizing the flow battery concept in the LiO2 system, a remarkable specific energy of 773 Wh/kg was achieved at the current density of 0.25 mA/cm2, accounting for the mass of the active materials (Li metal and O2), the separator, the electrolyte, and the positive electrode. Notably, the energy required to circulate the electrolyte during operation is minimal, amounting to just 1.3 % of the energy discharged.

The specific energy obtained in this study is significantly higher than most literature on LiO2 batteries. Many of these studies do not specify the weight of key battery components and do not report cell-level specific energy. However, our comparisons of areal capacity and current density relative to existing literature (Fig. S10) highlight the advantages of our active cell design. It is important to note that all data reported in the literature used specially designed and synthesized positive electrodes, while our study used cost-effective, commercially available positive electrodes.

Moreover, the calculated specific energy of the active battery in this study significantly exceeds that of state-of-the-art Li-ion and LiS batteries. Leading battery initiatives and consortia worldwide, such as BATTERY 2030+ in the European Union, the Battery500 Consortium in the USA, and Rising II in Japan, have set a specific energy target of 500 Wh/kg for energy storage (Li-ion and LiS). While this target is considered very aggressive at the cell level, the results from our work demonstrate the feasibility of achieving and even exceeding this specific energy target at the chemical level. Further improvements in cathode electrode design could significantly increase both areal and specific energy density.

Please note that cycle life, in addition to specific energy, is another important performance target in rechargeable battery research. While the number of cycles achieved in this study is significantly lower than that of Li-ion [40] or LiS batteries [41], the primary factor limiting cyclability is the degradation of Li metal. Since LiO2 and Li-air batteries are open systems, protecting lithium metal is much more challenging. Although this was not the focus of our study, future research on developing advanced membranes, SEI layers, and advanced electrolytes will be crucial in increasing the lifetime and cyclability of LiO2 batteries.

This flow battery concept has the potential to be applied to a wide range of porous electrodes and electrolytes. This study employs commercial electrodes as a proof of concept. However, customized electrodes with higher mass loadings can be fabricated to further improve the energy density and efficiency of the battery. Customized electrodes could use a variety of substrates (e.g., carbon paper, carbon cloth, carbon felts), carbon-based materials (e.g., carbon nanoparticles, graphene, carbon nanotubes), other cost-effective materials, and precious metals to enhance performance and balance the overall system cost. It could revolutionize metal-O2, metal-air, and metal-CO2 batteries by providing unprecedented high capacities and specific energy densities. The findings of this research pave the way for utilizing cost-effective commercially available materials to achieve significant breakthroughs in specific energy density, thereby offering substantial contributions to the energy storage community and fostering new avenues of research and development. The research team anticipates a series of new results based on this flow battery concept in the coming years.

Xianglin Li: Visualization, Project administration, Writing – original draft, Validation, Resources, Writing – review & editing, Software, Funding acquisition, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The author greatly appreciates the funding support by the Advanced Research Projects Agency-Energy (ARPA-E) PROPEL-1K program (Award DE-AR0001884) and the National Science Foundation (Award 1941083 and 2329821). The author also wants to acknowledge Helen Ma for conducting XPS and the Raman spectrum experiments.
